options.text: Write an email about a project delay
Write a professional email to my manager explaining that the Q2 report will be delayed because I'm waiting on data from finance

Write a professional email to my manager explaining that the Q2 report will be 3 days late because I'm waiting on data from the finance team. Include the delay reason, acknowledge the impact, and propose next steps. Keep it around 100-150 words.

Here's an email I sent last week about a schedule change — I'd like to match this tone and format:

"Subject: Design Review — Moved to Thursday

Hi Sarah, Quick heads-up — the design review is moving to Thursday. The vendor sent revised mockups late yesterday and I want to give the team a full day to review before we meet. I know this bumps against the sprint deadline, so I've blocked Friday morning as a backup. I'll send the updated mockups today. Let me know if Thursday works on your end. — [Your name]"

responses.response_weak:
Subject: Project Delay

Hi,

I wanted to let you know that there will be a delay with the project. We've run into some issues that are pushing back our timeline. I apologize for any inconvenience this may cause and will keep you updated as things progress.

Please let me know if you have any questions.

Best,
[Your name]
responses.response_medium:
Subject: Q2 Report Delay

Hi [Manager's name],

I wanted to give you a heads-up that the Q2 report is going to be delayed. I'm currently waiting on some key data from the finance team that I need to complete the analysis, and I haven't received it yet.

I've already reached out to their team to follow up and am hoping to get the data soon. I'll prioritize finishing the report as quickly as possible once I have what I need.

I apologize for the delay and will keep you posted on the timeline. Let me know if you'd like to discuss this further.

Best regards,
[Your name]
responses.response_strong:
Subject: Q2 Report — Delayed 3 Days

Hi Sarah,

Quick heads-up — the Q2 report is going to land about 3 days past our original deadline. I'm still waiting on the financial data from the finance team, and I can't finalize the analysis without it.

I know this may affect your planning, and I apologize for the timing. Here's where things stand:

- I've followed up with finance and they've confirmed delivery by **[date]**
- Once I have the data, I'll prioritize wrapping up the report
- **Revised delivery: [new date]**

I'll send you a quick status update tomorrow and let you know the moment it's ready.

— [Your name]

feedback.what_happened: The weak prompt produces a vague, generic delay email that could apply to any project — it doesn't mention the Q2 report, finance team, or timeline, leaving your manager guessing about details. The medium prompt gets the basics right with a more specific email, but the AI has to guess at tone and structure, producing something professional but generic. The effective prompt includes all the same context plus a real example email — and that's the game-changer. The AI picks up on the conversational tone ('Quick heads-up'), the concise structure, and the direct style from your sample, producing an email that actually sounds like something you'd write.
feedback.principle: Show what 'good' looks like — giving AI an example of your own writing produces output that sounds like you, not a template.
feedback.tip: When asking AI to write something, paste in an example of similar writing you've done before. Even a short sample gives the AI a concrete style to match instead of guessing at your tone — this is the difference between generic AI output and something that sounds like you.
